title: Pakistani Zarda (Sweet Rice)

ingredients.name: saffron strands soaked in 3 tbsp warm milk

subs.name: 1/4 tsp turmeric mixed in warm milk
subs.notes: gives colour; lacks saffron's floral aroma

subs.name: 50 g condensed milk, drizzled on top before serving

steps.text: Bring 800 ml water to a boil in a large pot with a pinch of salt and 2 of the cardamom pods. Add the soaked, drained basmati rice and boil for 7–8 minutes until 75% cooked — the grains should still have a very slight bite. Drain thoroughly in a colander.

In the same pot, heat ghee over medium heat. Add the remaining cardamom pods and fry for 20 seconds until fragrant. Add the sugar and 150 ml water, stirring until the sugar dissolves completely to form a light syrup. Allow to simmer for 3 minutes.
Add the parboiled rice to the sugar syrup and stir gently to coat every grain. Pour the saffron-milk over the top but do not mix — this creates streaks of deep yellow and white in the final dish.

Drizzle the rose water over the rice. Cover the pot with a damp cloth and then the lid to create a tight seal. Cook on the very lowest flame for 20 minutes on dum, allowing the rice to steam in the syrup until each grain is separate, glossy, and fully cooked.

While the rice is on dum, heat 1 tbsp of ghee in a small pan and fry the cashews, almonds, and raisins for 2 minutes until the nuts are golden and the raisins puff up. Remove from heat immediately to avoid burning.

Open the dum rice, fold the yellow and white layers gently with a wide spatula. Transfer to a serving platter and top with the fried dry fruits, crumbled khoya if using, and slivered pistachios. Serve warm or at room temperature.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: saffron → 1/4 tsp turmeric mixed in warm milk (gives colour; lacks saffron's floral aroma); khoya → 50 g condensed milk, drizzled on top before serving (adds sweetness and creaminess); ghee → butter (similar richness; slightly different aroma)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Zarda is nearly identical to north Indian zarda pulao and can be made in any Indian kitchen without changes. Saffron (kesar) from Kashmiri suppliers is widely available at Indian stores; the Pampore saffron of Kashmir is actually better quality than most available in Pakistan. Khoya (mawa) is sold fresh at mithai shops across India. Use a heavy aluminium pot with a tight lid for the dum step.
